content:
Let 
 S = 9 + 9² + ⋯ + 9²⁰¹⁰ = (9²⁰¹¹ – 9)/(9 – 1) = (9²⁰¹¹ – 9)/8. 
We check small primes p in turn:

1. p = 2:  
   9 ≡1 (mod 8) ⇒ 9²⁰¹¹ ≡1 (mod 8), so 9²⁰¹¹ – 9 ≡1–1=0 (mod 8), hence S is an integer and one checks S≡0 (mod 2).  
2. p = 3:  
   Every term 9ᵏ is divisible by 3, so 3|S.  
3. p = 5:  
   9≡4 (mod 5), so S≡∑ₖ₌₁²⁰¹⁰4ᵏ=(1005·4+1005·1)=1005·5≡0 (mod 5).  
4. p = 7:  
   9≡2 (mod 7), order 2|3 so ∑2ᵏ over 2010 terms breaks into 670 full 3‐cycles each summing to 0, whence 7|S.  
5. p = 11,13:  
   One checks similarly that the order of 9 mod 11 is 5, mod 13 is 3, both dividing 2010, so 11|S and 13|S.  
6. p = 17:  
   9 mod 17 has order 8, but 8∤2010.  In fact  
     S ≡∑ₖ₌₁²⁰¹⁰9ᵏ ≡5 (mod 17),  
   so 17∤S.

Thus the smallest prime failing to divide S is 

\boxed{17}.

 S = 9 + 9² + ⋯ + 9²⁰¹⁰ = (9²⁰¹¹ – 9)/(9 – 1) = (9²⁰¹¹ – 9)/8.

   9 ≡1 (mod 8) ⇒ 9²⁰¹¹ ≡1 (mod 8), so 9²⁰¹¹ – 9 ≡1–1=0 (mod 8), hence S is an integer and one checks S≡0 (mod 2).  
2. p = 3:  
   Every term 9ᵏ is divisible by 3, so 3|S.  
3. p = 5:  
   9≡4 (mod 5), so S≡∑ₖ₌₁²⁰¹⁰4ᵏ=(1005·4+1005·1)=1005·5≡0 (mod 5).  
4. p = 7:  
   9≡2 (mod 7), order 2|3 so ∑2ᵏ over 2010 terms breaks into 670 full 3‐cycles each summing to 0, whence 7|S.  
5. p = 11,13:  
   One checks similarly that the order of 9 mod 11 is 5, mod 13 is 3, both dividing 2010, so 11|S and 13|S.

   9 mod 17 has order 8, but 8∤2010.  In fact

\boxed{17}.
